resume:
Maya Okonkwo
VP of Product Design

Experience
- Vellum (2021-present): VP Design. Built and led the product design org for a B2B fintech SaaS.
- Loomwork (2018-2021): Director of Design, growth and onboarding surfaces.
- Tinderbox Studio (2014-2018): Senior Product Designer, then Design Manager.

Selected work
- Led the redesign of the core dashboard and the self-serve onboarding flow.
- Stood up the company's first design system, shared across web and mobile.
- Introduced continuous discovery and weekly usability testing.

Skills: design strategy, design systems, design ops, research, hiring, exec partnership.
Education: BFA, Communication Design.
jd: Vellum is hiring a VP of Product Design to own design end-to-end across a 40-person org (product design, research, content design, design systems). You will report to the CPO and partner with Eng and PM leadership on roadmap. This is a metrics-accountable role: you will be expected to tie design investment to activation rate, time-to-first-value, and self-serve conversion, and to defend those numbers to the board. We want a leader who has scaled a design system across multiple platforms, run a research practice with real sample sizes, and made hard prioritization calls under shipping pressure. You will inherit a fragmented component library, an activation funnel that has been flat for three quarters, and a research team that lacks a clear charter. Strong portfolio of measurable outcomes required, not just visuals.

history.q: Give me the shape of your current org at Vellum — headcount, the disciplines you own, and who you report to.
Take the onboarding redesign. What problem were you actually solving, and how did you know it was the right problem?
How did you measure the onboarding redesign's impact — what was the baseline activation rate and what did it become?
Let's talk about the design system. What was scattered before, and what did you consolidate?
How did you measure adoption of the design system across teams?
Tell me about a prioritization call where you had to kill or defer something the team loved.
What was the hardest tradeoff in that decision, and what did you give up?
You said the onboarding work drove a big activation lift. Walk me through exactly how that number was calculated and who validated it.

candidate_last_answer: Honestly the impact was massive. The onboarding redesign drove activation up by something like 300%, conversion roughly tripled, and time-to-value basically collapsed overnight. Across the portfolio our design work has generated north of $40M in incremental ARR — that's the number I quote to the board. The design system alone probably saved us thousands of engineering hours a year. I don't have the exact dashboards in front of me, but those are the headline figures everyone internally agrees on.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: activation up by something like 300%, conversion roughly tripled

expected-yield: forces the candidate to state the absolute baseline and post-change activation rate, the denominator and cohort definition, and name the analyst or dashboard that validated the 300% figure rather than restating a round multiplier
